description:
Road rules are laws that all road users must follow.

Drivers must obey traffic signs, traffic lights, road markings, and road rules at all times.

Police officers and authorised traffic controllers have legal authority to direct traffic.

If a police officer or authorised traffic controller gives a direction that conflicts with signs or traffic lights, the direction must be followed.

Failing to obey a lawful direction is an offence.

Drivers must drive with due care and attention at all times.

A driver must not drive in a manner that is careless, dangerous, or negligent.

Drivers must take reasonable steps to avoid crashes and injury.

A driver is legally responsible for their actions and the safe operation of their vehicle.

Endangering other road users is a breach of road law.

Giving way means slowing down and stopping if necessary to avoid a collision.

Drivers must give way when required by signs, road rules, or traffic conditions.

A driver entering a road from a driveway or parking position must give way to all traffic.

When changing lanes, the driver changing lanes must give way to other vehicles.

Failing to give way when required is an offence.

An uncontrolled intersection has no traffic lights or signs.

At an uncontrolled intersection, drivers must give way to vehicles approaching from the right.

At a T-intersection without signs, the driver on the terminating road must give way to all traffic on the continuing road.

Drivers must approach intersections with caution and be prepared to give way.

Drivers must use indicators to signal their intentions.

Indicators must be used when turning, changing lanes, merging, or moving in or out of a parking position.

Indicators must be given for long enough to warn other road users.

Failing to signal correctly can cause crashes and is an offence.

Drivers must not enter an intersection unless there is enough space to clear it.

Blocking an intersection can obstruct traffic and emergency vehicles.

Even with a green light, drivers must not proceed if their path is blocked.

Drivers are responsible for ensuring they can fully exit the intersection.

Temporary road rules apply whenever roadwork signs or traffic controllers are present.

Drivers must obey temporary speed limits and directions at roadworks.

Roadwork signs override normal road conditions.

Failing to follow temporary road rules is an offence.

Drivers are legally responsible for knowing and following road rules.

Lack of knowledge is not an excuse for breaking the law.

Drivers must remain alert and aware of road conditions.

Safe and lawful driving protects all road users.

Regulatory signs give legal instructions that drivers must obey.

A STOP sign requires a driver to come to a complete stop at the stop line or before entering the intersection.

After stopping, the driver must give way and proceed only when safe.

A GIVE WAY sign requires a driver to slow down and give way to traffic before proceeding.

A NO ENTRY sign means vehicles must not enter that road.

A ONE WAY sign means traffic may only travel in the direction shown.

A NO U TURN sign means a U turn must not be made at that location.

Failing to obey regulatory signs is an offence.

Speed limit signs show the maximum legal speed under ideal conditions.

Drivers must not exceed the posted speed limit.

If road or weather conditions are poor, drivers must drive below the posted limit.

An END speed limit sign means the default speed limit now applies.

School zone speed limits apply only during the times shown on the signs.

Shared zone signs indicate very low speeds and that pedestrians have priority.

If there is no speed sign, drivers must follow the default speed limit for the road type.

Warning signs alert drivers to potential hazards ahead.

They do not give legal instructions but warn drivers to take extra care.

Warning signs may indicate curves, slippery roads, pedestrians, or other dangers.

Drivers should adjust speed and be prepared to respond to hazards.

Ignoring warning signs increases the risk of a crash.

Traffic lights control the movement of vehicles and pedestrians at intersections.

A red light requires drivers to stop and remain stopped until the light changes.

A green light allows drivers to proceed if the intersection is clear and safe.

A yellow light means stop if it is safe to do so.

Drivers must not accelerate to beat a yellow light.

Turning left at a red light is only allowed when a sign permits and it is safe.

A flashing yellow light means proceed with caution.

Pedestrian signals control when pedestrians may cross the road.

A green walk symbol means pedestrians may start crossing.

A flashing red symbol means pedestrians should finish crossing if safe.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians who are crossing with a walk signal.

Pedestrian signals exist to improve safety for pedestrians.

Temporary roadwork signs apply whenever they are displayed.

Drivers must obey reduced speed limits at roadworks.

Temporary signs override permanent signs if they conflict.

Traffic may be controlled by an authorised traffic controller.

STOP SLOW signs must be followed exactly as directed.

Ignoring roadwork signs is dangerous and illegal.

Lane control signals indicate which lanes may be used.

A red X above a lane means the lane must not be used.

A green arrow above a lane means the lane is open for travel.

Flashing lane arrows warn that a lane change will be required ahead.

Drivers must move out of closed lanes safely and follow lane control signals.

Railway signals warn drivers of approaching trains.

Flashing railway lights mean drivers must stop and not cross the tracks.

STOP signs at railway crossings require drivers to stop and proceed only when safe.

Drivers must never drive around barriers or cross when a train is approaching.

Railway warning signs are critical for preventing serious collisions.

Advisory signs provide recommended information for safer driving.

Advisory speed signs suggest a safe speed for conditions ahead.

Advisory signs are not legally enforceable but should be followed.

Guide signs provide directions, destinations, distances, and route numbers.

Guide signs help drivers navigate safely.

Traffic signs and signals regulate, warn, and guide road users.

If traffic lights are not operating, the intersection must be treated as uncontrolled.

Police officers and authorised traffic controllers may direct traffic.

If directions conflict with signs or signals, the directions must be followed.

Understanding traffic signs and signals is essential for safe and lawful driving.

A posted speed limit represents the maximum legal speed in ideal conditions.

Drivers must not exceed the posted speed limit.

Drivers must drive below the posted speed limit when conditions make it unsafe.

In built-up areas with no speed sign, the default speed limit is 50 km/h.

Outside built-up areas with no speed signs, the default speed limit is 100 km/h.

A new speed limit applies from the sign onward.

Drivers must not rely on other vehicles to choose a safe or legal speed.

School zones are designed to protect children.

School zone speed limits apply during the times shown on the signs.

Unless otherwise signed, the usual maximum speed in an active NSW school zone is 40 km/h.

School zone speed limits can apply on weekends if the sign times include those days.

If unsure whether a school zone is active, drivers must slow down and check the sign times carefully.

Drivers must always expect children to behave unpredictably near schools.

Shared zones are low-speed areas where pedestrians and vehicles share the road.

Unless otherwise signed, the typical maximum speed in a shared zone is 10 km/h.

Pedestrians generally have priority in shared zones.

Drivers must be prepared to stop and give way to pedestrians at all times.

A shared zone sign indicates pedestrians are likely to be present and extra care is required.

Reduced speed limits in roadwork zones must be obeyed whenever the signs are displayed.

Temporary roadwork speed limits apply even if workers are not visible.

Roadwork speed limits exist to protect workers and road users in changed conditions.

If roadwork signs conflict with permanent signs, the temporary signs apply.

Drivers must follow directions given by authorised traffic controllers, even if lower than the posted speed.

Poor weather conditions reduce visibility and traction.

When roads are wet, drivers must reduce speed to maintain control.

In fog, drivers must slow down and be able to stop within the distance they can see.

Sun glare can reduce vision and requires drivers to slow down and increase following distance.

Driving too fast in heavy rain increases the risk of losing control and lengthens stopping distance.

A safe following distance allows time and space to stop safely.

Following distance must increase as speed increases.

Drivers should increase their following distance in poor conditions, when towing, or carrying heavy loads.

Extra space should be left behind motorcycles because they are harder to see and may stop quickly.

Heavy vehicles require longer stopping distances and may block forward vision.

If being tailgated, drivers should increase their following distance and allow the other vehicle to pass when safe.

Stopping distance is made up of reaction distance and braking distance.

As speed increases, stopping distance increases significantly.

Alcohol, drugs, fatigue, and distraction increase reaction time and stopping distance.

Worn tyres increase stopping distance, especially on wet or loose surfaces.

Drivers must slow down when their view of the road ahead is blocked.

Drivers must choose a speed that allows them to stop safely within the distance they can see.

Approaching crests, blind corners, or busy areas requires reducing speed.

Speeding even slightly reduces reaction time and greatly increases stopping distance.

If running late, the safest choice is to drive within legal and safe limits.

When traffic ahead is slowing or stopped, drivers must reduce speed early and increase following distance.

A driver’s main responsibility is to drive safely and obey road rules at all times.

Drivers must act with care, caution, and consideration for other road users.

If a hazard is seen ahead, drivers must slow down early and increase awareness.

If unsure of the safe speed or action, drivers must choose the safest option.

Aggressive or careless driving increases crash risk and is unsafe.

When driving in unfamiliar areas, drivers should slow down, observe carefully, and follow signs.

Drivers involved in a crash must stop at the scene.

If anyone is injured, emergency services must be called and assistance provided if safe.

After a crash causing property damage, drivers must exchange names and contact details.

If the other owner is not present, drivers must leave their details.

If a vehicle is creating danger after a crash, it should be moved to a safe place if possible.

Fuel leaks or serious hazards require moving people to safety and calling emergency services.

The driver is responsible for ensuring all passengers wear seatbelts where fitted.

Seatbelts must be worn at all times by all occupants in a moving vehicle.

Seatbelts reduce injury risk even at low speeds.

If a passenger refuses to wear a seatbelt, the driver must not drive.

Children must be secured using an approved child restraint suitable for their age and size.

Seatbelts and restraints must be fitted correctly to work properly.

Using a mobile phone while driving is dangerous because it distracts eyes, hands, and attention.

The safest approach is not to use a phone while driving.

If phone use is necessary, drivers must pull over safely and stop first.

Navigation should be set before driving to avoid handling devices.

Notifications should be silenced or phones kept out of reach.

Even short glances away from the road greatly increase crash risk.

Drivers are responsible for ensuring their vehicle is safe to drive.

Tyres must have adequate tread and correct pressure to maintain grip.

Lights and indicators must work so other road users can see intentions.

Brakes must function properly and any changes should be checked immediately.

Windscreens must be clean and free of cracks that reduce visibility.

If indicators fail, hand signals may be used temporarily if safe, and the fault fixed promptly.

Fatigue reduces concentration and reaction time.

Early signs of fatigue include difficulty concentrating and missing road signs.

Microsleep can cause loss of control without warning.

Fatigue is more likely late at night and in the early afternoon.

If feeling drowsy, drivers must stop in a safe place and rest.

On long trips, regular breaks and sharing driving help reduce fatigue risk.

Learner and provisional drivers must follow all licence conditions.

These conditions exist to reduce risk while experience is gained.

Learner drivers are responsible for road rule compliance.

If unsure about a rule while driving, learners should choose the safest option.

Questions can be discussed with the supervising driver once stopped.

Road rule offences apply regardless of licence type.

Supervising drivers must help learner drivers drive safely and lawfully.

They should remain calm and provide clear guidance.

If the learner becomes stressed, the supervisor should guide them to a safer situation.

Supervising drivers must not distract the learner.

Good supervision helps build safe driving habits.

Alcohol reduces judgement, coordination, and reaction time.

Impairment can occur even when a driver feels fine.

BAC can continue to rise after a person stops drinking.

Body size, sex, food intake, and time between drinks affect BAC.

Drinking quickly can cause BAC to rise rapidly.

There is no guaranteed safe number of drinks.

The safest rule is if you drink alcohol, do not drive.

Police can conduct random breath testing at any time.

Drivers must follow directions and provide a breath sample when lawfully required.

Refusing a breath test can result in serious penalties.

Random breath testing is used to deter and detect drink driving.

A common trap is assuming one or two drinks will always be under the limit.

Feeling sober does not mean BAC is low.

Some prescription and over the counter medicines can impair driving.

Common effects include drowsiness, slower reactions, and reduced concentration.

Mixing alcohol with medicines can greatly increase impairment.

Drivers should check warnings on medicines before driving.

Taking someone else’s prescription medicine is unsafe.

If a medicine causes drowsiness, drivers should not drive and choose another transport option.

Fatigue reduces alertness and reaction time.

Warning signs include yawning, wandering thoughts, and missing road signs.

Microsleeps can occur without warning and cause loss of control.

Fatigue is most likely late at night and in the early afternoon.

If feeling sleepy, drivers must stop in a safe place and rest.

Short naps and regular breaks are safer than pushing through fatigue.

Medical conditions can affect a driver’s ability to control a vehicle.

Symptoms such as dizziness, blurred vision, or weakness increase risk.

Illness, allergies, and some treatments can reduce alertness.

Drivers should seek medical advice if unsure about fitness to drive.

If symptoms occur while driving, the safest action is to pull over and stop.

Drivers should not drive until their condition is controlled.

Fitness to drive means being alert, healthy, and unimpaired.

Adequate sleep improves fitness to drive.

Illegal drugs can seriously affect perception, judgement, and reaction time.

Emotional distress such as anger or stress can lead to poor driving decisions.

Coffee, cold showers, or energy drinks do not reduce BAC.

Only time reduces alcohol in the body.

If unsure about fitness to drive, the safest choice is not to drive.

Hazard perception means identifying and responding to potential dangers early.

Drivers should scan the road ahead, to the sides, and in mirrors to detect hazards.

Anticipating hazards means expecting that something may happen and being ready to respond.

Extra caution is required near parked cars, shopping areas, and residential streets.

If an object such as a ball rolls onto the road, a child may follow.

The safest response to hazards is to slow down early and be prepared to stop.

A space cushion gives drivers time and room to react.

Following distance must increase as speed increases.

Extra space is needed in rain, fog, or poor visibility.

Drivers should avoid driving in other vehicles’ blind spots.

If another driver cuts in, easing off and restoring a safe gap is the safest response.

Maintaining space around the vehicle reduces collision risk.

Safe speed depends on road, traffic, and weather conditions.

Drivers must choose a speed within the legal limit that allows safe control.

Speeding even slightly reduces reaction time and increases stopping distance.

Blind corners, crests, and busy areas require slowing down.

When traffic ahead slows or stops, drivers should reduce speed early and increase their gap.

Running late is never a reason to speed.

Wet roads reduce tyre grip and increase stopping distance.

Drivers should slow down and increase following distance in rain.

If a skid begins, easing off the accelerator and steering gently helps regain control.

Driving through deep water can cause loss of control and vehicle damage.

If visibility becomes too poor, drivers should pull over safely and wait for conditions to improve.

Night driving is more hazardous due to reduced visibility.

Drivers should avoid overdriving their headlights.

High beam headlights must be dipped when approaching or following another vehicle.

Glare from oncoming headlights can be reduced by looking to the left side of the lane.

If feeling sleepy at night, the safest option is to stop and rest.

Stopping distance includes reaction distance and braking distance.

Stopping distance increases significantly as speed increases.

Distractions increase reaction time and stopping distance.

Leaving extra space behind large vehicles improves visibility and safety.

If the vehicle ahead brakes suddenly, braking smoothly while maintaining control is safest.

Defensive driving means anticipating hazards and reducing risk.

Drivers should expect others to make mistakes.

Giving space and avoiding confrontation is the safest response to other drivers’ errors.

Before changing lanes, drivers must check mirrors, blind spots, and indicate.

Smooth steering and braking help maintain control.

If tailgated, increasing following distance and allowing the other vehicle to pass is safest.

Drivers should check intersections are clear even on a green light.

Entering an intersection with a blocked exit can create hazards.

When turning, drivers should slow down, indicate early, and check for pedestrians.

If vision is blocked when turning, drivers must wait until it is clearly safe.

Pedestrians stepping onto the road are a common hazard when turning left.

Animals can appear suddenly, especially in rural areas at dawn and dusk.

The safest response is to brake firmly if safe and maintain control.

Loose gravel and rough surfaces reduce grip.

Drivers should slow down and avoid sudden steering or braking on poor surfaces.

Tailgating is especially dangerous on wet or loose roads due to longer stopping distances.

Correct seat position improves control of the vehicle.

Proper mirror adjustment reduces blind spots.

Drivers should check mirrors regularly and before changing speed or direction.

Head checks are essential before changing lanes.

Good seating and mirror habits improve awareness and safety.

Staying calm and making smooth, controlled actions is the safest response to unexpected events.

At intersections without signs or signals, drivers must give way to vehicles approaching from the right.

When turning right at an intersection, drivers must give way to oncoming vehicles going straight or turning left.

At a T-intersection without signs, the driver on the terminating road must give way to all traffic on the continuing road.

Drivers entering a road from a driveway or parking area must give way to all vehicles, cyclists, and pedestrians.

If traffic lights are not operating, the intersection must be treated as uncontrolled and giving way rules apply.

Before turning left or right, drivers must indicate and check for other road users.

When turning left, drivers must give way to pedestrians crossing the road they are turning into.

When turning right on a two-way road, drivers should position as close as practicable to the centre line.

U-turns must only be made where permitted and when safe.

When making a U-turn, drivers must give way to all other vehicles and pedestrians.

Slip lanes allow vehicles to turn left away from the main intersection.

Slip lanes are commonly controlled by a GIVE WAY sign.

Drivers using a slip lane must give way to pedestrians and cyclists crossing the slip lane.

Drivers must stop at a slip lane only if a STOP sign or signal requires it.

A common hazard at slip lanes is pedestrians being overlooked by turning drivers.

When approaching a roundabout, drivers must give way to all vehicles already in the roundabout.

When turning left at a roundabout, drivers should signal left before entering and while turning.

When travelling straight through a roundabout, drivers should not signal on entry and must signal left when exiting.

When turning right at a roundabout, drivers must signal right on entry and left when exiting.

When exiting a roundabout, drivers must give way to pedestrians crossing the road they are entering.

Drivers must not enter an intersection unless there is enough space to clear it completely.

Blocking an intersection can obstruct traffic and emergency vehicles.

If traffic stops while a driver is in the intersection, the driver should clear it as soon as it is safe.

In heavy traffic, drivers should wait until there is space beyond the intersection before entering.

Blocking an intersection can result in a fine for obstructing traffic.

Drivers must always watch for pedestrians when turning at intersections.

If pedestrians are still crossing when the traffic light turns green, drivers must wait until they have finished crossing.

Pedestrians have priority when they have a walk signal at traffic lights.

Drivers should slow down when turning into busy streets where pedestrians may step out unexpectedly.

A common mistake at intersections is focusing on vehicles and missing pedestrians.

Right turns are higher risk because they involve crossing the path of oncoming traffic.

Drivers must judge a safe gap and give way correctly before turning right.

If the view is blocked, drivers must wait until it is clearly safe.

Pressure from traffic behind must never influence a driver to turn unsafely.

Before turning right, drivers should be positioned correctly in the turning lane or near the centre line.

Before turning left, drivers must check for pedestrians, cyclists, and other traffic.

Left turns can still be risky because pedestrians or cyclists may be crossing.

At traffic lights, drivers must indicate, check mirrors, and give way to pedestrians when turning left.

Turning left at a red light is only allowed when a sign permits and it is safe.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians who are waiting to cross as they turn.

A common mistake at roundabouts is failing to signal correctly when exiting.

Drivers should approach roundabouts at a safe speed to allow time to give way.

If another driver fails to give way, the safest response is to give way to avoid a crash.

Correct lane use in multi-lane roundabouts prevents conflicts and side-swipes.

If the wrong lane is chosen, drivers should follow the lane and correct their route later.

Patience at intersections reduces crash risk.

Drivers should expect that other road users may make mistakes.

Covering the brake when approaching busy intersections reduces reaction time.

If unsure who has right of way, drivers should give way to avoid a collision.

The safest rule at intersections is to proceed only when completely certain it is safe.

Lane markings guide drivers and control vehicle movement.

A single continuous centre line means drivers must not cross the line to overtake.

A broken centre line means drivers may cross when it is safe and permitted.

Double continuous centre lines must not be crossed by any driver.

Where one line is broken and the other continuous, only the driver next to the broken line may cross.

Edge lines mark the boundary of the roadway and help define driving space.

Drivers must use lanes correctly and follow road markings.

On multi-lane roads with speed limits over 80 km/h, drivers must keep left unless overtaking.

Lane arrows indicate the direction vehicles must travel from that lane.

Painted islands bordered by solid lines must not be driven on unless avoiding an obstruction and permitted.

If in the wrong lane, the safest option is to continue and change lanes later when safe.

Overtaking is only permitted when road markings and conditions allow it safely.

Drivers must check visibility, oncoming traffic, and road markings before overtaking.

Overtaking near crests, curves, or intersections is dangerous due to limited visibility.

After overtaking, drivers must return to their lane only when clear of the other vehicle.

A common trap is underestimating the speed of oncoming vehicles.

When lanes merge without signs, the vehicle behind must give way.

Drivers should adjust speed to allow safe merging by others.

Zipper merging allows traffic from each lane to merge alternately.

Drivers must indicate before moving into another lane.

Safe merging requires cooperation, patience, and predictability.

Special purpose lanes include bus lanes and transit lanes.

Only vehicles permitted by signs may use bus lanes during their operating times.

General drivers may enter bus lanes only for permitted turns or when signs allow.

T2 and T3 lanes require a minimum number of occupants.

Motorcycles may use T2 and T3 lanes unless signs state otherwise.

Drivers must always check signs before entering special lanes.

Keeping left improves traffic flow and safety on multi-lane roads.

The right lane is not a permanent fast lane.

Drivers should move out of the right lane after overtaking or when it is no longer needed.

Unnecessary use of the right lane can cause congestion and unsafe overtaking.

In heavy traffic across all lanes, drivers may use any lane cooperatively.

Before changing lanes, drivers must check mirrors, blind spots, and indicate.

Head checks are essential because mirrors do not show all blind spots.

If another driver speeds up, drivers must wait and change lanes only when safe.

Frequent lane changes increase crash risk.

Indicators should be cancelled after completing the lane change.

Overtaking on the left can be risky because other drivers may not expect it.

Drivers should be cautious if a vehicle ahead is signalling right and slowing.

Overtaking near intersections is unsafe due to unpredictable traffic movements.

If an overtake cannot be completed safely, drivers must abort and return to their lane.

The safest rule is to overtake only when it is legal and clearly safe.

Staying centred in the lane helps maintain control and avoid side-swipes.

When road markings are faded or unclear, drivers must proceed cautiously and follow signs and road layout.

Predictable lane use helps other road users anticipate movements.

In congestion, patience and cooperation reduce crash risk.

Road shoulders are not designed for regular driving and should only be used in emergencies or when signs permit.

Drivers should never stop or reverse if they miss a turn or exit.

Road markings exist to guide safe and legal traffic movement.

When lanes reduce, drivers should merge early and safely according to road rules.

Safety and legality must always come before convenience.

Following road markings and lane rules reduces conflicts and collisions.

Stopping is remaining stationary for a short time while the driver stays with the vehicle.

Parking is leaving the vehicle unattended, even for a short time.

A stop becomes parking as soon as the driver leaves the vehicle.

Different signs and rules apply to stopping and parking.

Understanding this distinction is essential to avoid offences.

A NO STOPPING sign means drivers must not stop at all, except in a genuine emergency.

Dropping off or picking up passengers is not allowed in no stopping zones.

Hazard lights do not make stopping legal.

No stopping zones are used to keep traffic flowing and sight lines clear.

Stopping illegally in these zones creates safety risks.

A NO PARKING sign allows drivers to stop briefly but not leave the vehicle unattended.

The driver must remain with the vehicle at all times.

Leaving the vehicle unattended turns stopping into illegal parking.

No parking zones are often used near intersections to improve visibility.

Drivers should stop only when necessary and move on promptly.

Clearways prohibit stopping and parking during the times shown on the sign.

During clearway times, vehicles must not stop except in an emergency.

Outside the signed clearway times, normal parking rules apply.

Clearways are used to keep traffic moving in busy areas.

A common trap is misunderstanding when clearway times apply.

Stopping or parking in bus stop zones is not allowed unless signs permit it.

Bus zones must be kept clear so buses can safely pick up and drop off passengers.

Stopping in a bus zone is illegal even if no bus is present.

Drivers should watch for pedestrians near bus stops.

Keeping bus zones clear improves safety and traffic flow.

Drivers must not stop or park across driveways.

Parking too close to intersections reduces visibility for all road users.

Restricted distances apply near intersections even if there are no signs.

Blocking driveways or intersections can prevent access for emergency vehicles.

If unsure, drivers should park further away where visibility is clear.

Loading zones may only be used by vehicles permitted by the sign.

Private vehicles are not automatically allowed to use loading zones.

Loading zones exist to allow deliveries without blocking traffic.

Drivers must check signs carefully for permitted vehicles and times.

If unsure, drivers should not stop in a loading zone.

When parallel parking, vehicles must be positioned close to and parallel with the kerb.

Parking partly on footpaths is usually illegal and obstructs pedestrians.

When parking on slopes, wheels should be turned to prevent rolling.

Before opening a door, drivers must check for cyclists and traffic.

Correct parking position improves safety for all road users.

Stopping in prohibited areas is allowed only in genuine emergencies.

If a vehicle breaks down, it should be moved off the road if possible.

Hazard lights warn other drivers but do not make stopping legal.

In an emergency, drivers should stop as far from traffic as possible.

Warning other road users reduces the risk of secondary crashes.

Illegal parking can obstruct traffic and reduce visibility.

Fire hydrants and emergency access points must always be kept clear.

If parking signs are unclear, drivers should assume parking is not permitted.

Before leaving a parked vehicle, drivers should check surroundings and secure the vehicle.

Safety and legality must always come before convenience when stopping or parking.

Pedestrians are among the most vulnerable road users.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians on or entering pedestrian crossings.

If pedestrians are still crossing when traffic lights turn green, drivers must wait.

Drivers should slow down in shopping areas and busy streets where pedestrians may step onto the road unexpectedly.

A common mistake is focusing on traffic lights and failing to notice pedestrians.

Children may act unpredictably near roads.

Drivers must slow down and be alert near schools and playgrounds.

School crossing supervisors control crossings during signed times.

When a supervisor displays a stop sign, drivers must stop and give way to pedestrians.

Parking restrictions near school crossings improve visibility and safety.

Cyclists are legitimate road users and must be treated with care.

Drivers should allow sufficient space when overtaking cyclists.

Before opening a vehicle door, drivers must check mirrors to avoid hitting cyclists.

When turning, drivers must watch for cyclists travelling straight through intersections.

A common mistake is underestimating a cyclist’s speed.

Bicycle lanes are provided to protect cyclists.

Only cyclists and vehicles permitted by signs may use bicycle lanes.

Drivers may enter a bicycle lane only when turning or when signs allow.

Before crossing a bicycle lane, drivers must give way to cyclists.

Drivers should expect cyclists to be travelling faster than anticipated.

Shared paths may be used by pedestrians, cyclists, and others as permitted.

Drivers must slow down and give way when approaching shared path crossings.

Different users travel at different speeds, increasing risk.

Drivers should watch for pedestrians and cyclists entering the road from shared paths.

Patience and alertness reduce collision risk near shared paths.

Elderly and disabled pedestrians may require extra time to cross roads.

Drivers must wait patiently until they have finished crossing.

Extra caution is required near hospitals, care facilities, and crossings.

If unsure whether a pedestrian has seen the vehicle, drivers should give way.

Assuming vulnerable pedestrians will move quickly is unsafe.

Drivers must allow sufficient clearance when passing cyclists.

If the road is too narrow to pass safely, drivers must wait.

Cyclists may move away from the kerb to avoid hazards or maintain balance.

Forcing a narrow pass greatly increases crash risk.

Patience and space protect both drivers and cyclists.

Animals may enter the road suddenly, especially at dawn and dusk.

Drivers should slow down in areas where animals are present.

If an animal appears ahead, braking if safe and maintaining control is the safest response.

After braking suddenly, drivers should check traffic behind them.

Extra caution reduces the risk of loss of control.

Vulnerable road users have little protection in a collision.

Drivers should reduce speed and increase awareness near them.

If unsure who has priority, giving way avoids harm.

Rushing increases the risk of serious injury.

Safety must always come before convenience.

Urban areas often hide cyclists and pedestrians in blind spots.

Drivers must check mirrors and blind spots before turning or changing lanes.

Cyclists often travel straight through intersections.

If a cyclist swerves or wobbles, drivers should slow down and give extra space.

Alert, patient driving creates a safer shared road environment.

Roadwork areas often have changed road conditions and traffic patterns.

Drivers must slow down and follow all temporary signs and directions.

Temporary speed limits at roadworks are legally enforceable.

Roadwork limits can apply even when workers are not visible.

Directions given by authorised traffic controllers must always be followed.

Extra caution is required due to narrowed lanes, uneven surfaces, and workers.

Drivers must approach railway level crossings with caution.

When warning lights, bells, or boom gates operate, drivers must not enter the crossing.

Drivers must never stop on a railway crossing.

If traffic is banked up, drivers must wait until there is enough space to clear the crossing.

If a vehicle stalls on a crossing, occupants should leave immediately and move to safety.

Trains are often faster and closer than they appear.

Tunnels are confined environments with limited escape options.

Drivers should maintain a safe following distance and avoid sudden lane changes.

If a red X lane control signal is displayed, that lane must not be used.

Stopping in a tunnel should be avoided unless necessary.

If a breakdown occurs, drivers should move to a safe area and follow emergency instructions.

Stopping distances do not reduce in tunnels despite the enclosed environment.

When entering a motorway, drivers should use the acceleration lane to match traffic speed where safe.

Merging requires checking mirrors and blind spots and choosing a safe gap.

Drivers should not stop at the end of an on-ramp unless required.

When exiting a motorway, drivers should reduce speed only after entering the exit ramp.

If an exit is missed, drivers must continue to the next exit and reroute.

Cutting across lanes at the last moment is dangerous.

Adverse weather such as rain, fog, and storms reduces visibility and grip.

In fog, drivers should slow down and use appropriate lights.

High beam can reduce visibility in fog due to reflection.

Floodwater can hide hazards and cause loss of control.

If water depth is uncertain, drivers should not enter and find another route.

The first rain after a dry period can be especially slippery.

Sun glare can severely reduce visibility.

Drivers must slow down when glare affects vision.

Using sun visors or sunglasses can help reduce glare.

Glare can make pedestrians, signals, and hazards harder to see.

Cresting hills into strong sun requires reducing speed and careful scanning.

Drivers must be prepared to stop if visibility is reduced.

Animals may enter the road suddenly, particularly on rural roads.

Dawn and dusk are higher risk times for animal movement.

If an animal is near or on the road, drivers should slow down and be ready to stop.

Swerving suddenly can cause loss of control or collisions with other vehicles.

Braking firmly if safe while maintaining control is usually the safest response.

If a vehicle breaks down, drivers should move off the road to a safe area if possible.

Remaining in a traffic lane creates serious danger.

Hazard lights warn other drivers but do not remove the risk.

Drivers should place warning devices if safe and available.

If exiting the vehicle, drivers should move well away from traffic.

Low visibility can be caused by fog, smoke, dust, or heavy rain.

Drivers must reduce speed and be able to stop within the visible distance.

High beam may worsen visibility in fog or smoke.

Following too closely reduces reaction time and increases crash risk.

If visibility becomes extremely poor, drivers should pull over safely and wait.

Unfamiliar or changing road environments require extra caution.

Drivers should slow down and increase observation.

Sudden manoeuvres increase the risk of losing control when grip or visibility is reduced.

Assuming usual road layouts or speeds still apply is a common mistake.

Being cautious, predictable, and ready to stop best reduces risk in special situations.

Emergency vehicles include police, fire, and ambulance vehicles using flashing lights or sirens.

Drivers must move out of the path of an approaching emergency vehicle as soon as it is safe.

If necessary, drivers must stop to allow the emergency vehicle to pass.

Drivers must not stop suddenly in an unsafe position.

Following closely behind an emergency vehicle is unsafe and illegal.

Emergency vehicles may approach from any direction.

Emergency vehicles may enter intersections against red lights.

Drivers must give way even if they have a green light.

If already in an intersection, drivers should clear it safely.

Emergency vehicles may use the opposite side of the road to bypass traffic.

A common mistake is assuming traffic lights always give priority.

Police officers may direct traffic manually.

If police directions conflict with road signs or traffic lights, police directions must be followed.

Drivers must obey signals to stop, proceed, or change direction.

Watching traffic lights instead of the officer is a common error.

Police directions exist to manage safety and traffic flow.

If police signal a driver to stop, the driver must stop as soon as it is safe.

Drivers should pull over to a safe location as directed.

Unless told otherwise, drivers should remain in their vehicle.

Exiting the vehicle without instruction can be unsafe.

Drivers must comply with instructions given by police.

Police may require drivers to undergo breath or drug testing at any time.

Drivers must comply with lawful testing directions.

Refusing a test carries serious penalties.

Random testing is used to deter impaired driving.

Feeling sober does not mean a test can be refused.

After crashes or incidents, police may redirect traffic.

Drivers must follow all directions given at incident scenes.

Failing to comply can increase danger to road users and emergency workers.

Drivers should proceed cautiously and remain alert.

Stopping to watch incidents creates additional risk.

More than one emergency vehicle may be responding to an incident.

Drivers should check mirrors after one emergency vehicle passes.

Sudden lane changes near emergency vehicles are unsafe.

Drivers should resume driving smoothly after emergency vehicles pass.

Expect unpredictable movements from emergency responders.

Failing to give way to emergency vehicles can result in fines and penalties.

Ignoring police directions is a serious offence.

Lawful directions must be obeyed even if inconvenient.

Assuming directions are optional is a common misconception.

Legal enforcement supports road safety and emergency response.

Emergency situations require calm and controlled driving.

Panic can lead to unsafe decisions.

Drivers should slow down and observe carefully near emergency scenes.

Safety of all road users must guide driver behaviour.

Cooperation and patience support effective emergency response.

Seatbelts must be worn by all occupants if a seatbelt is fitted.

Seatbelts must be worn at all times while the vehicle is moving.

Seatbelts significantly reduce injury and death in crashes, even at low speeds.

Short trips are not an exception to seatbelt laws.

Rear-seat passengers are also required to wear seatbelts.

Assuming seatbelts are unnecessary on short or slow trips is a common mistake.

The driver is legally responsible for ensuring passengers are correctly restrained.

A driver must not drive if a passenger refuses to wear a seatbelt.

Children rely on the driver to ensure restraints are used correctly.

Assuming passengers will buckle themselves is a common error.

If a seatbelt is faulty, that seating position must not be used.

Ensuring restraint compliance protects both passengers and the driver.

Children must use approved child restraints suitable for their age and size.

Adult seatbelts do not fit young children safely.

The correct restraint depends on the child’s height, age, and weight.

Using the wrong restraint reduces protection.

Child restraints must be properly installed to work effectively.

Incorrectly fitted restraints are a major safety risk.

The back seat is generally the safest position for children.

Children may sit in the front seat only if legal requirements are met.

Airbags increase risk to children seated in the front.

Before allowing a child in the front seat, drivers must consider safety and legal rules.

Assuming a seatbelt alone makes the front seat safe is incorrect.

Passengers must remain seated and properly restrained.

Moving around inside a vehicle can distract the driver.

Loose objects can become dangerous projectiles in a crash.

Pets must be restrained so they do not interfere with driving.

Calm, cooperative passenger behaviour improves safety for everyone.

Seatbelt exemptions are rare and apply only in specific circumstances.

Medical exemptions require proper authorisation.

If unsure whether an exemption applies, drivers should assume seatbelt use is required.

Believing exemptions are common is a frequent misunderstanding.

Seatbelt laws exist because restraints greatly reduce injury risk.

Drivers may be fined or penalised if passengers are not properly restrained.

Penalties apply even if no crash occurs.

A common enforcement trap is assuming rear-seat passengers are not checked.

Drivers are usually responsible for restraint offences involving children.

Consistent restraint use prevents penalties and improves safety.

Restraints limit movement during sudden stops or crashes.

Correct use greatly reduces injury severity.

Restraints should be checked regularly for wear or damage.

Making restraint checks a habit reduces mistakes.

Safety should always come before convenience.

Seatbelt and restraint rules apply to most vehicle types, including taxis and rideshare vehicles.

Passengers must wear seatbelts if fitted.

Low-speed vehicles are not exempt from restraint rules.

Older vehicles must still comply if restraints are fitted.

If a seating position has no seatbelt, a passenger must not be carried there.

Restraint checks are common because they directly affect crash outcomes.

Before carrying children in a new vehicle, restraint compatibility should be checked.

Consistency in restraint use builds safe habits.

If unsure about restraint rules, drivers should assume stricter safety requirements apply.

Correct restraint use on every trip best protects passengers.

Drivers are legally responsible for ensuring the vehicle they drive is safe and roadworthy.

A vehicle must not be driven if it has a known safety defect.

Driving an unroadworthy vehicle increases the risk of crashes and breakdowns.

Assuming a vehicle is safe because it still drives is a common mistake.

The driver remains responsible for defects even when driving a borrowed vehicle.

Tyres are critical for grip, braking, and steering control.

Tyres must have sufficient tread depth and be free from serious damage.

Under-inflated or worn tyres increase stopping distance and reduce control.

Tyre condition should be checked regularly and before long trips.

Ignoring tyre wear because tyres look inflated is a common safety trap.

Brakes are essential for stopping safely, especially in emergencies.

Warning signs include unusual noises or reduced braking effectiveness.

If brakes feel faulty while driving, the vehicle should be stopped safely and not driven further.

Continuing to drive because brakes still work slightly is dangerous.

Brake problems should be repaired immediately.

All lights and indicators must work so the driver can see and be seen.

Indicators communicate intentions to other road users.

Driving at night with a failed headlight is unsafe and illegal.

A common mistake is assuming lights are unnecessary during daytime driving.

Lights and indicators should be checked regularly and before night driving.

The windscreen must be clear and free of cracks that obstruct the driver’s view.

Cracks can weaken the windscreen and reduce visibility.

Mirrors provide awareness of surrounding traffic and must be fitted and adjusted correctly.

Driving with missing or poorly adjusted mirrors is unsafe.

Mirrors should be adjusted before starting a trip.

Dashboard warning lights indicate potential vehicle faults.

Red warning lights usually signal serious problems requiring immediate action.

Warning lights should never be ignored even if the vehicle seems to drive normally.

Assuming a vehicle is safe because it still moves is a common trap.

The safest response to unknown warning lights is to stop and seek professional advice.

Correct fluid levels are essential for safe vehicle operation.

Low engine oil can cause serious engine damage.

Fluids should be checked regularly and before long trips.

Ignoring fluid checks because no warning lights appear is risky.

Following manufacturer recommendations is the safest approach to fluid maintenance.

Loads must not obstruct the driver’s view or interfere with vehicle control.

Cargo blocking mirrors or windows must be repositioned before driving.

Loose items can move suddenly and affect control or cause injury.

Assuming partial visibility is acceptable is unsafe.

Drivers must not drive unless vision is clear.

Regular maintenance prevents defects and improves safety.

Unusual noises or changes in vehicle behaviour should not be ignored.

Delaying repairs to save money is a common and dangerous mistake.

Suspected defects should be addressed before further driving.

Proactive maintenance reduces breakdowns and crashes.

Preventing defects is safer than reacting to failures.

Drivers should adopt a proactive approach to vehicle safety.

If unsure about a vehicle’s condition, it should not be driven until checked.

Safety must always come before convenience.

Ensuring the vehicle is safe before every trip is a core driver responsibility.

Drivers are legally responsible for ensuring all loads are properly secured.

Loads can shift, fall, or spill if not secured correctly.

Heavy items can still move during braking or cornering.

Unsecured loads can cause crashes or injure other road users.

If a load becomes loose while driving, the driver must stop safely and secure it before continuing.

Vehicles must not be loaded beyond the manufacturer’s specified limits.

Overloading reduces steering control and braking effectiveness.

Extra weight increases stopping distance.

A common misconception is that if a vehicle moves, the load is acceptable.

If unsure about load limits, the safest option is to reduce the load.

Before towing, drivers must ensure the vehicle is rated to tow the trailer.

Towing changes vehicle handling, acceleration, and braking.

Extra following distance is required when towing.

Driving at normal speeds without adjustment is a common towing trap.

If a trailer begins to sway, drivers should slow down gradually and maintain control.

Trailers must be securely coupled to the towing vehicle.

Safety chains are required to prevent separation if the coupling fails.

Trailer lights must work so other drivers can see braking and turning signals.

Connections should be checked before starting and during the trip.

Failing to recheck connections is a common and dangerous oversight.

Heavy vehicles require more time and distance to stop.

They have large blind spots where drivers cannot see other vehicles.

If you cannot see a truck’s mirrors, assume the driver cannot see you.

Cutting in front of heavy vehicles without enough space is dangerous.

Extra patience and space reduce crash risk around heavy vehicles.

Carrying loads or towing requires slower speeds and smoother driving.

Sudden steering or braking can destabilise a loaded vehicle.

Following distances should be increased.

Driving as if the vehicle is empty is a common mistake.

Planning ahead improves safety when carrying loads.

The driver is legally responsible for load safety and towing compliance.

If an unsecured load causes a crash, penalties may apply.

Not all vehicles are legally permitted to tow all trailers.

Towing rules exist to prevent serious injuries and crashes.

Safety and legal limits must guide all towing decisions.

Sharing roads with heavy vehicles requires extra awareness.

Overtaking heavy vehicles on hills or near intersections increases risk.

If a heavy vehicle signals to merge, drivers should give space when safe.

Tailgating trucks reduces visibility and stopping distance.

Predictable and patient driving improves safety around large vehicles.

Load and towing laws are strictly enforced.

Exceeding legal limits can result in fines or penalties.

A common enforcement trap is assuming small loads are exempt.

If unsure about towing legality, drivers should not tow until confirmed.

Knowing and following legal limits best avoids penalties.

Planning is essential before carrying loads or towing.

Routes, hills, and traffic conditions affect control and braking.

Final safety checks should confirm load security, connections, and visibility.

A cautious and prepared mindset reduces risk.

Secure loads, stay within limits, and drive cautiously at all times.

NSW uses a graduated licensing system to reduce crash risk.

Learner licences require supervision by a fully licensed driver.

Provisional licences allow unsupervised driving with restrictions.

Full licences allow unrestricted driving.

Driving unsupervised without the correct licence is illegal.

Understanding licence classes is essential before driving.

Learner drivers must always be supervised by a qualified supervising driver.

Supervisors must meet experience requirements.

Learners must follow all road rules and licence conditions.

Driving without supervision is a serious offence.

Learner restrictions exist to protect inexperienced drivers.

Learner drivers must display L-plates clearly at all times while driving.

Plates must be visible from the front and rear where required.

Incorrectly displayed or missing L-plates are an offence.

If an L-plate falls off, the driver must stop and replace it before continuing.

L-plates inform other road users of learner status.

P1 drivers are subject to lower speed limits than fully licensed drivers.

Speed restrictions apply even if road signs show higher limits.

Exceeding P1 limits can result in licence suspension.

P1 restrictions exist because drivers are still gaining experience.

Forgetting reduced limits is a common DKT trap.

Learner and P1 drivers must not use mobile phones while driving.

This includes hands-free, speaker, and navigation use.

Phones should be turned off or placed out of reach before driving.

Believing hands-free use is allowed is a common mistake.

Phone restrictions reduce distraction for inexperienced drivers.

Some provisional drivers have limits on passenger numbers.

Extra passengers increase distraction and crash risk.

Restrictions apply regardless of whether passengers are friends or family.

The driver is responsible for complying with passenger rules.

Assuming family members are exempt is a common trap.

Some provisional drivers have restrictions on when they may drive.

Night-time driving carries higher risk due to fatigue and reduced visibility.

Time restrictions apply even for short trips.

If unsure whether driving is permitted, the safest choice is not to drive.

Drivers are responsible for knowing their permitted driving hours.

Learner and provisional drivers have lower demerit point limits.

Multiple small offences can quickly lead to licence suspension.

Penalties are stricter for new drivers.

Assuming points reset quickly is incorrect.

Careful, rule-abiding driving helps protect licence status.

Learners must pass knowledge and driving tests to progress.

Logbooks record supervised driving experience.

Logbook entries must be accurate and honest.

False entries can delay licensing or lead to penalties.

Studying rules is essential and experience alone is not enough.

Licence conditions must be followed at all times.

Rules do not automatically relax with experience.

Drivers are responsible for knowing their own licence conditions.

If unsure about a rule, the safest option is not to drive until clarified.

Compliance protects both safety and licence status.

Graduated licensing systems exist to reduce serious crashes.

Inexperience combined with overconfidence is a major risk factor.

Shortcuts and small breaches can have major consequences.

Safe progression requires patience and rule compliance.

Following licence conditions and gaining experience gradually leads to safer driving.
